kneeFlexion.critical: Your {{leg}} knee is almost locked at {{angle}} degrees. Bend your knees more to absorb terrain and maintain balance. Aim for 90-140 degrees of flexion.
kneeFlexion.warning: Your {{leg}} knee is getting straight at {{angle}} degrees. Try to maintain a more athletic bend for better shock absorption.

shoulderAlignment.critical: Your shoulders are rotated {{angle}} degrees from the board. Keep your shoulders more aligned with the board direction for better edge control and stability.
shoulderAlignment.warning: Your shoulders are slightly off-axis at {{angle}} degrees. Try to keep your upper body aligned with your direction of travel.

stanceWidth.warning: Your stance looks very narrow. Widen your feet to about shoulder-width for better stability and board control.

summary.needsAttention: Several issues need attention — focus on the critical items below.
summary.solid: Solid technique overall, with a few areas to refine.
summary.decent: Decent form with some areas that need work.

kneeFlexion.title: Bend Your Knees More
kneeFlexion.whatIsWrong: Your legs are too straight. Riding with locked-out knees makes it hard to absorb bumps, steer, and stay balanced.
kneeFlexion.howToFix: Keep a soft, athletic bend in both knees throughout the run. Imagine sitting into an invisible chair — your shins should press lightly against the front of your boots.
kneeFlexion.drillTip: Practice "1000 turns" at low speed: on each turn, exaggerate the knee bend until your thighs burn slightly. This builds muscle memory for the correct position.

shoulderAlignment.title: Align Your Shoulders with the Board
shoulderAlignment.whatIsWrong: Your shoulders are rotated too far open (facing downhill). This forces your hips out of alignment and makes edge-to-edge transitions sluggish.
shoulderAlignment.howToFix: Keep your shoulders roughly parallel to the board. Your lead hand should point toward the nose; your rear hand stays over the tail. Think "quiet upper body."
shoulderAlignment.drillTip: Grab both ends of a pole or stick while riding and keep it parallel to the board. If the stick rotates, your shoulders are opening up.

stanceWidth.title: Widen Your Stance
stanceWidth.whatIsWrong: Your feet are too close together. A narrow stance reduces stability and makes it harder to engage your edges effectively.
stanceWidth.howToFix: Set your bindings at shoulder width or slightly wider. Your weight should be evenly distributed between both feet with your knees tracking over your toes.
stanceWidth.drillTip: On flat ground, stand in your board and have a friend push you gently from different directions. Widen your stance until you feel stable against every push.

snowboard: The first snowboard was invented in 1965 by Sherman Poppen, who called it the "Snurfer."
Snowboarding became an Olympic sport in 1998 at the Nagano Winter Games.
Pro snowboarders can reach speeds over 200 km/h (125 mph) on steep slopes.
The world's longest snowboard run is over 16 km at Alpe d'Huez, France.
Proper knee flexion absorbs up to 70% of terrain impact, reducing injury risk.
Keeping your shoulders aligned with the board improves edge control by 40%.
Professional snowboarders train off-season with skateboarding and surfing.
The halfpipe was originally inspired by skateboard half-pipes in the 1980s.
skiing: Skiing dates back over 5,000 years — the oldest ski ever found is from Norway.
The fastest recorded speed on skis is 254.958 km/h (158 mph).
Alpine skiing has been an Olympic sport since the 1936 Winter Games.
Proper parallel ski technique reduces quad fatigue by up to 30%.
running: The marathon distance (42.195 km) was standardized at the 1908 London Olympics.
Elite marathon runners maintain a cadence of about 180 steps per minute.
Proper running form can reduce injury risk by up to 50%.
The human body is uniquely adapted for long-distance running among primates.
golf: The longest drive ever recorded was 515 yards by Mike Austin in 1974.
A golf ball has between 300 and 500 dimples that improve aerodynamics.
The average golf swing generates about 2,000 pounds of force.
Tiger Woods' club head speed exceeds 125 mph during a drive.
yoga: Yoga originated in ancient India over 5,000 years ago.
There are over 84 classic yoga asanas (poses) in traditional practice.
Regular yoga practice can improve flexibility by 35% in just 8 weeks.
Yoga has been shown to reduce cortisol levels by up to 25%.
home_workout: Bodyweight exercises have been used for military fitness training for centuries.
A proper push-up engages over 200 muscles simultaneously.
Squats are considered the king of all exercises for lower body strength.
Plank exercises can strengthen over 20 different muscle groups at once.
general: Video analysis can improve athletic performance by 15-25% through technique correction.
AI pose estimation processes up to 30 frames per second for real-time feedback.
Athletes who review video of their performance improve 2x faster than those who don't.
Proper body alignment reduces injury risk by up to 50% across all sports.
Joint angle analysis is used by Olympic coaches across 15+ sports disciplines.
The human body has over 360 joints — our AI tracks the 10 most critical for your sport.
